1: The Lake of Gennesaret (= the Sea of Galilee) is where the fishing economy of the region operated. The crowds pressing around Jesus to hear "the word of God" (ton logon tou theou = the word of God -- not just "his teaching") frames the scene in theological terms before the miracle: God's word is being proclaimed to Israel's working people beside Israel's waters. The scene deliberately echoes Ezekiel 47:9-10: Ezekiel's vision of the river flowing from the temple making the Dead Sea alive with fish, where "fishermen will stand along the shore." The miracle that follows activates this imagery.
2: Two boats at the water's edge, fishermen washing their nets -- the detail of net-washing after a fruitless night (Luke 5:5) makes the subsequent miracle's impossibility vivid. Clean nets, put away, on boats pulled up to shore: everything signals the end of the working period. Simon's boat being available becomes the staging point for a public teaching that ends with a private miracle that transforms the fisher into a follower. The double structure (public word, private miracle, personal call) is the pattern of vocational calling throughout Luke-Acts.
3: Jesus uses Simon's boat as a floating platform for teaching -- a detail that makes the subsequent miracle personal: Simon has served Jesus's teaching mission before being asked to serve him in the miraculous catch. The boat as pulpit resonates with the ship/boat symbolism in early Christianity (the church as the ark of salvation, with Christ as the helmsman -- a common patristic image). More immediately, it places Simon in a position of proximity and service before the theopanic encounter: he has been Jesus's host (Luke 4:38), now his boat-provider. The relationship precedes the call.
4: "Put out into deep water (eis to bathos = into the depth) and let down the nets for a catch" -- the instruction to put out into the deep after a fruitless night reverses the normal pattern of Galilean fishing (shallow-water, night-time netting). The "deep" is also the theological register of the Psalms: "Out of the depths I cry to you, LORD" (Psalms 130:1). Jesus commands an entry into the deep where human effort has produced nothing, which is exactly where the divine provision appears. The miracle tests faith in a word that contradicts experience and professional expertise.
5: "Master (epistatē), we've worked hard all night and haven't caught anything. But because you say so, I will let down the nets" -- Simon's response is the model of obedient faith under contrary evidence. The "because you say so" (epi de tō rēmati sou = but at your word) is explicitly word-based obedience: Simon is acting not on his own fishing judgment but on Jesus's word alone. This is the same structure as the centurion's faith (Luke 7:7: "say the word and my servant will be healed") -- the recognition that Jesus's word alone is sufficient cause for action, regardless of contrary experience. This is the faith the resurrection will demand on a cosmic scale.
6: "They caught such a large number of fish that their nets began to break" -- the miraculous catch of fish has been read typologically since the earliest commentators (Cyril of Alexandria, Ambrose): the fish = the souls drawn into the church's net through apostolic proclamation. John 21:11 records a second miraculous catch of 153 fish (after the resurrection), also connected explicitly to the apostolic mission. The breaking nets suggest a fullness that exceeds the capacity of the existing vessel -- the new-wine/new-wineskins saying (Luke 5:37-38) will articulate this same pattern: the new reality bursts old containers.
7: The signal to partners and the two boats almost sinking from the weight is the communal dimension of apostolic work: Peter is not the only fisherman-disciple (James and John, sons of Zebedee, are partners). The "almost sinking" (hōste bythizesthai = so that they were going under) dramatizes the abundance: the miracle's fullness threatens the boats designed to contain it. This is an enacted parable of the Kingdom's abundance: the harvest is greater than any existing vessel can hold. The Lukan theme of the Kingdom's expansiveness recurs throughout: Luke 5:39, Luke 13:18-21, Luke 14:22-23 (still room).
8: "When Simon Peter saw this, he fell at Jesus' knees and said: Go away from me, Lord; I am a sinful man" -- the theopanic response (falling, confession of unworthiness, request for distance) is the pattern of Isaiah's vision (Isaiah 6:5: "Woe to me! I am ruined! For I am a man of unclean lips") and Gideon's (Judges 6:22: "I have seen the angel of the LORD face to face! I will die!"). Peter recognizes that he is in the presence of the holy God and that such proximity is dangerous for a sinful human. "Lord" (kyrios) -- Peter's first use of the resurrection title for the earthly Jesus in Luke. The recognition precedes the calling: Peter knows who Jesus is before Jesus explains his mission.
9: "For he and all his companions were astonished at the catch of fish they had taken" -- the astonishment (thambos = overwhelming wonder/amazement) is the human response to theophany throughout Luke (Luke 1:12; Luke 4:36; Luke 8:25; Luke 9:43; Luke 24:22). The astonishment is not merely psychological surprise at a remarkable event but the specific response of creatures encountering the holy. The miraculous catch of fish is a theophany -- the self-disclosure of the creator Lord in the midst of the ordinary. The fishing context emphasizes this: the Lord of creation is revealing his sovereignty in the domain of these men's ordinary work.
10: "Don't be afraid; from now on you will fish for people" -- the "do not fear" (mē phobou) is the standard divine reassurance after theophany (Genesis 15:1; Joshua 1:9; Isaiah 41:10; Luke 1:13, 30; Luke 2:10; Acts 18:9; Revelation 1:17). The commission transforms Simon's vocation: the skills of catching (zōgrōn = catching alive, taking alive, used of both fish and men in Greek) become the skills of apostolic proclamation. The implicit promise: the miraculous abundance of the fish catch -- thousands where Peter caught nothing -- will be repeated in the harvest of human souls. Acts 2:41 (3,000 on Pentecost) is the first fulfillment.
11: "So they pulled their boats up on shore, left everything and followed him" -- the immediacy and completeness of the response (aphentes panta = having left everything) is the decisive mark of a call. The leave-everything motif runs through the disciple calls: Levi at Luke 5:28, the rich ruler's failure at Luke 18:22-23, and Peter's declaration at Luke 18:28. "Following" (ēkolouthēsan = they followed, the imperfect of continuous action) inaugurates the discipleship relationship: they walk where he walks, learn what he teaches, see what he does. Everything from this point onward in the Gospel is experienced as followers of the one who called them.
12: "A man covered with leprosy" fell on his face and begged: "Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean." The double address to Jesus's will and power is the perfect prayer: "if you are willing" (ean thēlēs = if you desire, choose to) acknowledges sovereignty; "you can make me clean" (dynasai katharisai = you are able to cleanse me) acknowledges power. Levitical law required the leper to maintain distance (Leviticus 13:45-46); this man has prostrated himself at Jesus's feet. The act is both a violation of the purity code and an act of extraordinary faith: he comes near to the one whose holiness, he trusts, will cleanse rather than condemn him.
13: "Jesus reached out his hand and touched the man. I am willing, be clean!" -- the touch (hapsamenos = having touched him) is the Christological act: Jesus touches the untouchable. Leviticus 5:3 specified that touching a person with an infectious skin disease made the toucher unclean. Jesus reverses the direction of contagion: his holiness flows into the leper rather than the leper's uncleanness flowing into Jesus. This is the logic of the Incarnation itself: the Holy One entering the unclean world does not become unclean but makes the unclean clean. The Incarnation is the supreme touch -- the Word becoming flesh to cleanse what flesh had defiled.
14: "Go, show yourself to the priest and offer the sacrifices that Moses commanded for your cleansing, as a testimony to them" -- Jesus upholds the Mosaic purification procedure (Leviticus 14:2-32). The healed leper's priestly verification is the legitimate re-entry into the covenant community. The phrase "as a testimony to them" (eis martyrion autois = as a witness/testimony to them) is ambiguous: testimony to the priests of the healing? or testimony against them of what they are missing? The same phrase appears in Luke 9:5 (shaking dust off feet "as a testimony against them"). Jesus honors the Torah while his action testifies that the era of the Law's promises is being fulfilled.
15: "Yet the news about him spread all the more, so that crowds came to hear him and to be healed of their sicknesses" -- the growth pattern despite (or because of) Jesus's instruction for silence anticipates the unstoppable spread of the Gospel in Acts. Acts 5:14: "more and more men and women believed in the Lord and were added to their number." Acts 6:7: "the word of God spread... the number of disciples increased rapidly." Acts 12:24: "the word of God continued to spread and flourish." The Lukan pattern: the Word of God advances irresistibly, not by human strategy, but by the Spirit's sovereign work through human proclamation.
16: "But Jesus often withdrew to lonely places and prayed" -- the pattern of Jesus's prayer retreat shapes the entire Lukan portrait: prayer before baptism (Luke 3:21), before choosing the Twelve (Luke 6:12), before the Transfiguration (Luke 9:28), at Gethsemane (Luke 22:41-44). The prayer of Jesus is not a devotional extra but the structural reality of his relationship with the Father that funds all ministry. The "lonely places" (tas erēmous = the deserted places) echo the wilderness of the temptation: Jesus returns to the place of dependence and solitary communion whenever the crowd's demands multiply. His authority comes from this intimacy with the Father.
17: The gathering of "Pharisees and teachers of the law" from "every village of Galilee and from Judea and Jerusalem" at a house where Jesus teaches creates the setting for the forgiveness controversy. The religious leadership of the nation assembles before the one who will forgive and heal in the same act. "The power of the Lord was present for him to heal" (dynamis kyriou ēn eis to iasthai auton = the power of the Lord was present for healing through him) -- the healing is the Lord's power working through the Son, not merely a natural ability. The presence of the Pharisees functions as the necessary witnesses for what follows: the declaration of the Son of Man's authority to forgive sins.
18: Friends carrying a paralyzed man -- the four carry the helpless one to the only one who can help him. The paralytic cannot come to Jesus himself; he must be carried by others whose faith acts on his behalf. This is an enacted parable of intercession: the friends' faith literally gets him to Jesus. "They tried to take him into the house to lay him before Jesus" -- the physical barriers are the barriers of a world that doesn't make it easy to get to Jesus. The friends will not be stopped. Their persistence anticipates the parable of the widow (Luke 18:1-8): faith does not give up at the first obstacle.
19: Unable to get through the crowd, "they went up on the roof and lowered him on his mat through the tiles into the middle of the crowd, right in front of Jesus" -- the Palestinian house had a flat roof accessible by exterior stairs; the "tiles" suggest a Hellenized roof construction (Mark 4:4 has "digging through the roof"). The lowering of the paralytic into the middle of the crowd is a feat of determination and improvisation. The dramatic entrance through the roof means the paralytic literally descends to Jesus -- a Christological image for the humble approach to the Son of Man who came down from heaven (John 6:38: "I have come down from heaven").
20: "When Jesus saw their faith, he said: Friend, your sins are forgiven" -- the first response is not healing but forgiveness. Jesus addresses the paralytic as "friend" (anthrōpe = man, literally -- a somewhat familiar/warm address). The forgiveness (apheōntai = have been forgiven, perfect passive: the forgiving has been done and stands) precedes the healing. This sequence is the theological argument: the deepest human need is not physical restoration but forgiveness of sins. The healing will follow as the sign that forgiveness has actually occurred. Jesus treats the moral/spiritual condition as primary and the physical condition as secondary -- the opposite of what the crowd and the religious authorities expect.
21: "Who is this fellow who speaks blasphemy? Who can forgive sins but God alone?" -- the Pharisees' theological objection is technically correct: only God can forgive sins (Isaiah 43:25: "I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions"; Mark 2:7 parallel). Their error is not in the theology but in the Christological conclusion they fail to draw. If Jesus forgives sins and the healing confirms it, then their premise leads to a conclusion they will not accept: this man is God. The objection that "only God can forgive" is an implicit Christological argument that cuts both ways -- if Jesus forgives sins and is authenticated, then he is who he says he is.
22: "Jesus knew what they were thinking and asked: Why are you thinking these things in your hearts?" -- the knowledge of thoughts (eidōs tas enthumēseis = knowing the inner reasonings/thoughts) is a divine attribute in the OT (1 Chronicles 28:9: "the LORD searches every heart and understands every desire and every thought"; Psalms 139:2). The silent objection in the Pharisees' hearts is known to Jesus as clearly as spoken words. This is a revelation of God: the one who knows hearts is present. The subsequent question turns the Pharisees' logic against them: they are asked to decide which statement is easier to make, which will expose the deeper question of authority.
23: "Which is easier: to say, your sins are forgiven, or to say, get up and walk?" -- the question is epistemologically sharp. To say "your sins are forgiven" is empirically unverifiable: no one can observe whether forgiveness has actually occurred. To say "get up and walk" is immediately falsifiable: if the man doesn't walk, the word has failed. Jesus uses the verifiable act (healing) as the sign that confirms the unverifiable act (forgiveness). The logic: if I can command the physical body and have it obey, this demonstrates the authority behind the spiritual declaration. The miracle is not the main act but the evidence for the main act.
24: "But I want you to know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" -- the Son of Man's authority to forgive sins is a Christological claim of the highest order. Daniel 7:13-14 gives "the Son of Man" universal dominion; Jesus here claims that this universal dominion includes authority that belongs exclusively to God -- the forgiveness of sins. The combination of the Daniel title with the divine prerogative of forgiveness is the central offense of the passion: Luke 22:69-70 (Son of Man + Son of God = blasphemy charge). Jesus enacts here what the Sanhedrin will condemn him for later -- and the healing is the ongoing sign that his claim is true.
25: "Immediately he stood up in front of them, took what he had been lying on and went home praising God" -- the immediacy and completeness of the healing is the authentication Jesus claimed. The man who was carried in on a mat leaves carrying it: the reversal is total. "Praising God" (doxāzōn ton theon = glorifying God) is the proper response to the forgiveness and healing. It directs the praise to the Father rather than to Jesus, consistent with Jesus's own practice (Luke 10:21: "I praise you, Father"). The man's healing and praise together demonstrate what the forgiveness of sins produces in those who receive it: restored mobility and worship.
26: "Everyone was amazed and gave praise to God. They were filled with awe and said: We have seen remarkable things today" (eidamen paradoxa sēmeron = we have seen extraordinary/paradoxical things today) -- paradoxa (paradox, extraordinary things, things beyond expectation) is the word for events that contradict normal expectations. The healing of the paralytic is a paradox in both the physical and theological sense: a man who couldn't move walked; a man who could only be forgiven by God was forgiven by a Galilean teacher. The "today" (sēmeron) echoes Luke 5:21 ("today this scripture is fulfilled") -- the eschatological today of Jesus's ministry is when extraordinary things happen.
27: "After this, Jesus went out and saw a tax collector by the name of Levi sitting at his tax booth. Follow me, Jesus said to him" -- Levi's call parallels Simon's (Luke 5:1-11): both are called from their normal occupation by a direct command with no prior preparation. The tax collector's booth (telōnion = the customs/tax-collection post) was a position of social infamy (tax collectors were regarded as traitors and extortioners). The call of Levi demonstrates the same principle as the leper's cleansing: Jesus extends his touch to the untouchable. The community Jesus is forming includes those whom polite Jewish society excluded -- anticipating the church that Acts describes.
28: "And Levi got up, left everything and followed him" -- "left everything" (katēlipen panta = left behind all things) parallels the fishermen's call (Luke 5:11: "left everything"). The completeness of the response is emphasized in both cases. For Levi the cost is economic as well as social: his tax booth represents ongoing income that cannot be recovered. This is more costly than the fishermen's nets, which can be returned to. The fact that Levi hosts a great banquet (Luke 5:29) suggests he has the resources to make this sacrifice. Generosity in the face of loss is the response of those who understand what they have received.
29: Levi "held a great banquet for Jesus at his house, and a large crowd of tax collectors and others were eating with them" -- the banquet is the proper response to grace: celebration, hospitality, and inviting others to share. The guest list -- "tax collectors and others" (hamartōloi = sinners, as the Pharisees call them in Luke 5:30) -- is the first visible sign of the new community Jesus is forming. The table fellowship with sinners is the enacted parable of the Kingdom: the great banquet of Luke 14:15-24 where the originally invited refuse and the poor/lame/blind/sinners are brought in. The Messiah's table is not for the self-righteous but for those who know their need.
30: "But the Pharisees and the teachers of the law who belonged to their sect complained to his disciples: Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?" -- eating together (commensality) in the first-century Mediterranean world was an act of social identification: you eat with those who are your equals or your clients. To eat with "sinners" was to identify with them and implicitly to endorse their behavior. The Pharisees' complaint is socially intelligible: the table fellowship is a public statement about who belongs to the covenant community. Jesus's answer redefines the covenant community's boundaries.
31: "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick" -- the physician metaphor for Jesus appears in 4:23 as a challenge (physician, heal yourself) and here as a self-description. Jesus identifies himself as the doctor who goes to the sick. In Jewish tradition, God is the physician (Exodus 15:26: "I am the LORD who heals you"). Jesus's self-description as physician places him in the role that God claimed for himself in the Exodus. The logic is gracious: the doctor's presence is required by those who need him, not by those who don't. Jesus is present among sinners because sinners are where the physician is needed.
32: "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance" -- the explicit statement of mission: Jesus came (elthon = I came, the aorist of a completed coming from somewhere) to call sinners. The combination of "call" (kalesai = to summon, invite) and "repentance" (metanoian = a change of mind/direction) frames the Gospel as an invitation. The "righteous" are those who, like the Pharisees, do not recognize their need. The calling of sinners fulfills Isaiah 55:7: "Let the wicked forsake their ways and the unrighteous their thoughts. Let them turn to the LORD, and he will have mercy on them." Jesus is the voice crying in the wilderness, calling the lost to return.
33: "John's disciples often fast and pray, and so do the disciples of the Pharisees, but yours go on eating and drinking" -- the fasting question is a question about eschatology: fasting is an act of mourning, waiting, and longing for the Kingdom's arrival. John's disciples and the Pharisees fast because the Kingdom has not yet arrived. Jesus's disciples feast because the bridegroom is present (Luke 5:34). The controversy about fasting is a controversy about whether the messianic age has begun. Jesus's answer ("the bridegroom is with them") is the implicit claim: the age of the Kingdom has begun. The feast is appropriate; fasting would be a denial of the eschatological reality.
34: "Can you make the friends of the bridegroom fast while he is with them?" -- the bridegroom imagery is drawn from the OT's description of God as Israel's husband (Isaiah 54:5: "your Maker is your husband"; Hosea 2:16-20; Jeremiah 31:32). John the Baptist had identified himself as the friend of the bridegroom who rejoices at the bridegroom's voice (John 3:29). Jesus is the bridegroom -- a divine title -- who is now present with his people. The feasting that his presence requires is the eschatological feast of the Kingdom. Paul will use the same imagery (2 Corinthians 11:2: "I promised you to one husband, to Christ") and Revelation will culminate it (Revelation 19:7: "the wedding of the Lamb").
35: "But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; in those days they will fast" -- "taken away" (apathē = is taken away from them, removed) is the language of sudden violent removal (cf. Isaiah 53:8 LXX: "his life was taken from the earth"). Jesus predicts his violent removal from the disciples' presence -- the cross -- as the event that will reactivate fasting. The post-ascension church fasts (Acts 13:2-3; Luke 14:23) because the bridegroom has been taken, though they fast in hope rather than despair: the wedding feast awaits (Revelation 19:9). The verse frames the passion as the interval between the first and second coming.
36: "No one tears a piece out of a new garment to patch an old one" -- the new/old garment parable is the first of three analogies for the relationship between Jesus's ministry and the existing structure of Judaism. The new cloth tears when used to patch the old: both are destroyed -- the new coat is ruined, the old coat is not properly fixed. Jesus's teaching is not a patch for a worn-out religion. The covenant he brings is not a reform of the existing covenant but a new covenant -- which is why Jeremiah 31:31 uses the word "new" (hādāshāh = new, unprecedented, of a different kind). The new patch/old garment = the incompatibility of the new covenant with the old legal-religious structure.
37: "And no one pours new wine into old wineskins. Otherwise, the new wine will burst the skins; the wine will run out and the wineskins will be ruined" -- old dried wineskins had lost the elasticity to expand with fermenting new wine; they would burst. New wineskins were still flexible. The fermentation process of new wine is the Spirit's active, expansive work (Acts 2:13: the crowd at Pentecost says the disciples are "filled with new wine"). The new covenant -- the Spirit poured out, forgiveness given freely, Jew and Gentile one community -- cannot be contained within the rigid legal structures of the old covenant. The wineskin imagery announces the structural incompatibility, not the abolition of the old but its completion and transcendence.
38: "No, new wine must be poured into new wineskins" -- the positive statement completes the analogy. The new wine requires new vessels: the new covenant requires new structures (the church as the new covenant community), new practices (baptism and the Lord's Supper replacing circumcision and Passover as the covenant sign and covenant meal), and new understanding (the Spirit illuminating the Scriptures to reveal Christ throughout). Paul makes the same argument in 2 Corinthians 3:6-14 and Galatians 4:21-31: the old and new covenants are not compatible in the sense that the old cannot contain what the new has inaugurated.
39: "And no one after drinking old wine wants the new, for they say: The old is better" -- this saying is unique to Luke and is often overlooked. It is not a condemnation of those who prefer the old but a realistic observation about human inertia: those accustomed to the old covenant's ways (the Pharisees, the disciples of John) will not immediately prefer the new. The old is familiar and comfortable; the new requires adjustment. The saying is not cynical but realistic -- it explains why the Pharisees resist Jesus's new wine rather than dismissing them as simply wicked. The resistance to Jesus is partly the resistance of all humans to genuine newness, which is always costly to receive.
